1: David and the military commanders (śārê haṣṣāḇāʾ) set apart for service the sons of Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun ‘who were to prophesy (hannibbêʾîm) with lyres, harps, and cymbals.’ The Hithpael participle hannibbêʾîm (those who prophesy) applies the prophetic vocabulary to musicians. The Hithpael of nāḇāʾ (to prophesy) describes an ecstatic or inspired state (cf. 1 Sam 10:5-6 where the prophetic band plays instruments and prophesies). The temple musicians’ work is not merely artistic performance but a form of inspired proclamation — ‘a manifestation of the Spirit analogous to prophetic speech’ (cf. 1 Cor 14:26 ‘when you come together... each one has a... revelation’).
2: From Asaph’s line: Zaccur, Joseph, Nethaniah, and Asarelah — Asaph’s sons, serving under Asaph’s direction (ʿal-yêḏê ʾāsāp). The idiom ʿal-yêḏê (lit. ‘upon the hands of’) means ‘under the supervision/direction of.’ Asaph prophesied (hannabbêʾ ʿal-yêḏê hammelek) ‘under the direction of the king.’ The chain of authority flows from YHWH → David → Asaph → his sons. Asaph is the author of Psalms 73–83 in the canonical Psalter; his role as temple musician and prophet explains why psalms bear his name.
3: From Jeduthun’s line: his six sons serve under Jeduthun’s direction ‘with the lyre, to prophesy in giving thanks and praising YHWH’ (ʿal-yĕdê yêdûtûn bannĕḇîyim bĕḵinnôr lāmôḏōt ûlāhallêl YHWH). The prepositional phrase lāmôḏōt ûlāhallêl (to give thanks and to praise) defines the prophetic content of the music: thankgiving (yāḏāh, Hiphil infinitive) and praise (hālal, Piel infinitive). Temple prophetic music is functionally oriented toward tôḏāh (thanksgiving offering/declaration) and hll (praise) — the two poles of the Levitical worship vocabulary.
4: From Heman’s line: his sons Bukkiah, Mattaniah, Uzziel, Shebuel, Jerimoth, Hananiah, Hanani, Eliathah, Giddalti, Romamtiezer, Joshbekashah, Mallothi, Hothir, Mahazioth. These fourteen names have been noted by scholars as forming, with some emendation, a prayer-poem: ‘I have been favored [by God]; have mercy on me, Lord; have mercy on me; Thou art my God, I praise Thee, I raise up [the oracle]; Thou sittest amid adversity; I see [visions] abundantly; and I spoke, by the grace of God, for the exaltation of the Horn.’ If this reading is correct (it is debated), Heman’s fourteen sons’ names are encoded liturgical language.
5: ĥêmān ḥôzeh hammelek — ‘Heman the king’s seer.’ ḥôzeh is the participle of ḥāzāh (to see, behold in vision) — a seer receives visual prophetic revelation. The same term describes Gad (2 Sam 24:11; 1 Chr 21:9), Asaph (2 Chr 29:30 ‘Asaph the seer’), and Jeduthun (2 Chr 35:15 ‘Jeduthun the seer’). All three temple musician-leaders are seers. ‘Given according to God’s word (dibrê hāʾĕlōhîm) to exalt his horn (lĕrûm qeren).’ rûm qeren (to raise the horn) is the idiom for bestowing strength and honor: the horn raised high = exaltation (cf. Ps 89:17; 92:10; 1 Sam 2:10 ‘YHWH will exalt the horn of his anointed’). God gave Heman fourteen sons to exalt YHWH’s glory.
6: ‘All these sons served under their father’s direction (ʿal-yêḏê ʾăḇîhem) in music for the LORD’s house, with cymbals (bĕmĕṣiltāyim), lyres (ḇinnĕḇālîm), and harps (ûḇĕkinnôrôt).’ The three instruments enumerated — cymbals, lyres, harps — are the characteristic temple orchestra. The term mĕṣiltayim (cymbals) appears only in Chronicles and Ezra-Nehemiah for the specifically Levitical cymbal-instrument (as distinct from ṣĕlṣĕlîm used in other contexts). Ps 150:4-5 employs the same instruments in a concluding doxology.
7: ‘Including their skilled fellow musicians (mĕḇînîm) who were trained in the LORD’s songs (mĕlummĕḏîm šîr YHWH), the total was two hundred eighty-eight.’ mĕḇîn (discerning, understanding, skilled) is the Hiphil participle of bîn (to understand, discern). The 288 comes from 24 courses × 12 members each; the mathematical structure mirrors the 24 priestly courses. mĕlummĕḏîm (trained, taught) is the Pual participle of lāmad (to learn, teach) — the same root as the noun talmîḏ (disciple/student) in the following verse.
8: ‘They cast lots for their duty assignments, regardless of rank (kĕqāṭōn kêgāḏôl), whether teacher or student (mêḇîn ʿim-talmîḏ).’ talmîḏ (disciple, student) is one of the rarest words in the OT — appearing only here and in Isa 8:16 (‘bind up the testimony, seal the law among my disciples’). The term that becomes central in NT usage (the Twelve as Jesus’ mathētai, Gk. translation of talmîḏ) has its OT root in temple music education. The lot ensures that the divine appointment falls ‘whether great or small, teacher or student’ — the lot is democratic in the sense that it reflects YHWH’s choice alone.
9: The first lot fell to Joseph from Asaph’s family (yôsēp libnê-ʾāsāp) — he and his brothers and sons, twelve. The organizational unit of each course is twelve (the number of the tribes, the complete Israel). The total of 24 × 12 = 288 encodes both the complete liturgical cycle (24) and the complete Israel (12). Each course serves for one week in a two-week rotation pattern (24 courses × 2 = 48 weeks; the remaining weeks fall at the three pilgrimage festivals when all priests/Levites serve together).
10: The second lot fell to Gedaliah (gĕdalyāhû) — he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Gedaliah means ‘YHWH is great.’ The name is also borne by the ill-fated governor of Judah after the Babylonian conquest (2 Kgs 25:22-25; Jer 40-41). The musician Gedaliah and the governor Gedaliah share a name that expresses the same theological confession; the name’s meaning endures through both uses.
11: Third lot: Zaccur (zakkûr), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Zaccur means ‘remembered’ (Niphal participle of zākar) — the one whom [YHWH] remembers. The same name appears among the gatekeepers (26:2) and in the post-exilic period (Neh 3:2; 10:12; 13:13). The lot-assignment formula (third lot / name / family size twelve) will repeat with minor variation through v31.
12: Fourth lot: Izri (yiṣrî), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Izri does not appear in the earlier Jeduthun list (v3, where the parallel slot has ‘Zeri’ ṣerî). The variation (Izri/Zeri) is likely a spelling variant: in Hebrew the letters yod-zayin-resh-yod can be confused with zayin-resh-yod. The LXX and other textual witnesses show variation here; the variant names are the same course.
13: Fifth lot: Nethaniah (nĕtanyāhû), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Nethaniah means ‘YHWH has given.’ The same name is borne by Ishmael son of Nethaniah, who assassinated Gedaliah the governor (2 Kgs 25:25; Jer 40:14). The Levitical musician and the violent Ishmael share the same patronym; the name represents a common Second Temple-period theological affirmation.
14: Sixth lot: Bukkiah (buqqiyyāhû), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Bukkiah means ‘proven/tried by YHWH’ (from bāqaʿ or bāqaq). The name is unique to this musical family in the OT.
15: Seventh lot: Jesharelah (yĕšareʾêlāh), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. The name does not appear elsewhere in this exact form. In the earlier list (v2) Asaph’s son is called ‘Asarelah’ (ʾăśarʾêlāh), which is likely the same name with a different spelling of the theophoric element. The variant spellings within the same chapter reflect the fluidity of pre-standardized Hebrew orthography.
16: Eighth lot: Jeshaiah (yĕšaʿyāhû), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Jeshaiah means ‘YHWH is salvation’ — the same semantic content as Isaiah and Joshua/Jesus. The theophoric name group yāšaʿ (to save) + YHWH produces the vocabulary of Israel’s salvation hope.
17: Ninth lot: Mattaniah (mattanyāhû), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Mattaniah means ‘gift of YHWH.’ This is the original name of King Zedekiah before Nebuchadnezzar changed it (2 Kgs 24:17). The Levitical musician and the last king of Judah share the same birth name; Mattaniah = ‘YHWH’s gift’ while Zedekiah = ‘YHWH is my righteousness.’
18: Tenth lot: Shimei (šimʿî), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Shimei means ‘YHWH has heard.’ The name appears for many OT figures including the Benjaminite who cursed David (2 Sam 16:5-13) and the Levite who led post-exilic revival in Ezra 10:23.
19: Eleventh lot: Azareel (ʿăzarʾêl), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Azareel means ‘God has helped’ (same as Azarel or Azriel). In v2 Asaph’s son is listed by a different name for this slot, suggesting that Azareel may be an alternate name or that the course took the name of its original head’s family designation rather than his birth-name.
20: Twelfth lot: Hashabiah (ḥăšaḇyāhû), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Hashabiah means ‘YHWH has considered/reckoned’ (from ḥāšaḇ, to think, plan, reckon). The name appears 12 times in Chronicles, Ezra, and Nehemiah for various Levitical officials — it was clearly a common name in Levitical families of the post-exilic period.
21: Thirteenth lot: Shubael (šûḇāʾêl), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Shubael means ‘return to God’ or ‘captive of God.’ This is identified in 23:16 as the head of Gershom’s (Moses’s son’s) line — the musician Shubael descends from Moses’s grandson, making this musical course carry the Mosaic lineage into the musical division.
22: Fourteenth lot: Mattithiah (mattityāhû), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Mattithiah means ‘gift of YHWH’ (from nātan + Yāh, differently constructed from Mattaniah). Mattithiah appears in Ezra 10:43 as one who married a foreign woman and agreed to put her away, and in Neh 8:4 as one who stood beside Ezra as he read the law — a significant liturgical role.
23: Fifteenth lot: Jeremoth (yĕrêmôt), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Jeremoth means ‘heights’ or ‘high places.’ In v3’s parallel list of Jeduthun’s sons, the corresponding name is ‘Jeshaiah’ — either a different son was named to this course in the lot assignment, or the names represent variants.
24: Sixteenth lot: Hananiah (ḥănanyāhû), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Hananiah means ‘YHWH is gracious.’ Among the most common OT theophoric names, Hananiah appears for the three friends of Daniel (Hananiah = Shadrach in Dan 1:6-7), showing that the name bridges the musical and the prophetic streams of Israelite tradition.
25: Seventeenth lot: Joshbekashah (yošbeqāšāh), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. The name is unusual and its meaning uncertain; it may mean ‘dwelling in hardship’ or ‘sitting in a hard place.’ It appears only in this musical context.
26: Eighteenth lot: Hanani (ḥănānî), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Hanani means ‘gracious’ or ‘YHWH is gracious.’ Hanani the seer rebuked King Asa for trusting Syria rather than YHWH (2 Chr 16:7-10) and was imprisoned for it; Nehemiah’s brother Hanani brought news of Jerusalem’s broken walls (Neh 1:2). The name’s frequency reflects its theological content.
27: Nineteenth lot: Mallothi (mallôtî), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Mallothi means ‘I have spoken’ or ‘utterance’ — a verb-form name from millêl (to speak). The speaking-name for a musician who serves in prophetic musical proclamation has an internal coherence: the musician speaks YHWH’s word through the medium of sacred song.
28: Twentieth lot: Eliathah (ʾĕlyātāh), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Eliathah means ‘my God has come’ or ‘God appears.’ The name encodes the theology of divine presence — God comes, God is present — which is the fundamental premise of the temple itself.
29: Twenty-first lot: Hothir (hôtîr), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Hothir means ‘abundance, surplus, excess’ (from yātar, to leave over, be abundant). The name of abundance for one of the temple’s musicians gestures toward the worship theme of overflowing gratitude.
30: Twenty-second lot: Giddalti (giddaltî), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Giddalti means ‘I have magnified/exalted’ (Piel perfect of gāḏal: to make great, magnify). The first-person verb as a name is a testimony-form: ‘I have magnified [the Lord].’ Ps 34:3 ‘magnify YHWH with me’ (gaddêlû YHWH ʾittî) uses the same root in the imperative.
31: Twenty-third lot: Mahazioth (maḥăzyôt); twenty-fourth lot: Romamtiezer (rômamtîʿezer). Romamtiezer is the most distinctive name in the list — it means ‘I have exalted his help’ or ‘exaltation of help.’ The root rûm (to exalt) + ʿezer (help) combines the exaltation-vocabulary of v5 (lĕrûm qeren, to exalt the horn) with the help-vocabulary of the divine name ‘Eliezer’ (my God is help) in 23:15. The twenty-fourth course closes the musical cycle on a note of exaltation and divine assistance — both themes central to Levitical temple worship.
